channel.title: Data-Governance on Athan Dial - Portfolio
channel.description: Recent content in Data-Governance on Athan Dial - Portfolio

item.title: Influence Comes From Decision Infrastructure
item.description:
Context

I took a reactive data work team and made it a strategic partner in drug discovery investment decisions over 18 months. The cost of staying in “service desk” mode was decision-integrity risk, burnout, and missed portfolio leverage: we were informing choices worth tens of thousands of dollars per round plus months of downstream lab work without any seat at the table.

The inherited operating model was simple: stakeholders sent ad-hoc requests (“pull hit rates for this target,” “how many compounds passed the filter?”) and we delivered fast, accurate outputs. That responsiveness created three compounding failure modes.

item.title: Warehouse Discipline Without a Data Team
item.description:
Context

The compound nomination engine and executive dashboards were hanging off raw Athena tables, so every mis-joined column risked steering six-figure wet-lab schedules. Advanced applied AI experiments were running on top of ad-hoc SQL. Analysts stitched assay results, procurement logs, and vendor catalogs together differently every week, so an enrichment chart was a suggestion rather than a fact. Modeling peers called out that a single typo in those long queries could swing nomination priorities, yet no one owned a transformation layer. Without lineage, knowledge lives in a system or in someone’s head, so regression analysis, procurement automation, and leadership reports kept contradicting each other. Every compound-level question turned into a Slack thread plus a copied SQL snippet that no one trusted.

item.title: A Biologist Caught What No Automated System Did
item.description:
Context

Two ML model configurations — one trained on a full compound dataset, one trained on a filtered subset — were producing nearly identical predictions. A biologist noticed. There were no automated data quality checks at any stage of the pipeline. No assertions, no monitoring, no alerting. The only reason this failure surfaced at all was that a domain expert recognized a pattern that didn’t make scientific sense.

That’s the starting condition: a failure that was invisible to every system we’d built, caught by someone paying attention.
